The Baldwin County Historical Society

Baldwin County

History in Stories

Andy Talbert & AI

↓

Chapter One

The First People

The Muscogees, the Alabamas, and the Sacred Neutral Ground

Long before Europeans set foot on the shores of Mobile Bay, the land we know as Baldwin County was sacred ground — a Neutral Ground where the Tensas, the Alabamas, the Choctaws, the Creeks, and the Seminoles met to plan the relationships of their tribes. It was, as one historian described, "the most free and easy, altogether informal, strip of land found anywhere." The eastern shore of the Bay held everything these peoples needed, save one thing: the means to cross to Mobile.

The Muscogees — known to Americans as the Creek Indians — traced their origins to a migration stretching back centuries. From tradition, this once-most-powerful tribe said they had originally crossed to America from Asia, finally settling in northwestern Mexico. When Hernando Cortez and Spanish troops battled Montezuma's forces, the Muscogees lost many warriors and resolved to seek another country.

They took up a line of march eastward, crossing great streams in the order of their clans: the Wind family first, followed by the Bear, the Tiger, and down to the humblest. Their army was led by the Tustennugee, the war chief. They struck the Red River, built a town, and came into conflict with the Alabamas — a separate tribe traveling east from Mexico. After battles and retreats, the Muscogees conquered the Alabamas, who fled to the Alabama and Tallapoosa Rivers and built their capitol where Montgomery now stands.

"They found a charming region, rich in soil, navigation and remote from enemies, and made permanent homes." — The Muscogees or Creek Indians from 1519 to 1893, by Dr. Marion Elisha Tarvin

By 1620, the Muscogees had gained firm foothold in the region. They advanced to the Ockmulgee, the Oconee, and the Ogechee, built a town where Augusta, Georgia now rests, and overcame all the Indians of Georgia. Under the influence of Bienville, the French Governor, the Muscogees and Alabamas became lasting friends. By 1540, when De Soto and his army passed through Alabama, the Muscogees were living on the Ohio River, and they had heard of "strange people" passing through.

In 1533, the Spanish adventurer Lope de Oviedo wandered along the Gulf Coast and wrote in his journal of "many nuts on the banks of the rivers" — what the Indians ate in their season, "coming from 20 to 30 leagues round about." Notes from De Soto's expedition in 1541 confirmed wild fruits were abundant, "great stores of a new kind of nut with soft shells and a form like a bullet." The pecan — native to this land — had fed indigenous peoples along the Gulf Coast since time immemorial, long before the Spanish gave it a name.

The Creek Wars and the Fall

The Creek Wars of 1813–1814 brought a violent end to the Muscogee way of life in this region. Brothers Adam and Alexander Hollinger of Baldwin County served as soldiers under General Andrew Jackson in the Creek War, fighting in all his battles — including Pensacola. They were comrades of the celebrated Sam Dale and Jerry Austill, heroes of the famous Canoe Fight on the Alabama River.

The massacre at Fort Mims in August 1813 — just north of today's Baldwin County — shocked the frontier settlements. Peter Randon, a man who was part Indian, later petitioned Congress for payment of losses sustained when "hostile Creek Indians murdered his father, and that nearly every relative he had were murdered in the massacre of Fort Mims." The entire account survives in a letter from Monroeville dated April 4, 1856, testifying to the lasting wound that battle left in the memories of families across the region.

By 1893, the world the Muscogees had known for centuries was gone. What remained were place names — the Tensaw, the Tombigbee, Perdido — and the pecan orchards first described by Spanish adventurers, still bearing fruit along the rivers of Baldwin County.

"By whatever name pecans have been known through the years, they have been synonymous with the land called Baldwin County for a long, long time."
— Kay Nuzum, Published in The Independent, September 26, 1979

Early Timeline of Baldwin County

1519
Spanish ContactMuscogees and Creek peoples establish presence across the Gulf Coast. Spanish adventurer Lope de Oviedo notes native pecan use along the rivers.
1541
De Soto's ExpeditionNotes from the expedition record abundant wild pecans, "great stores of a new kind of nut with soft shells."
1787
Spanish CensusPedro de Favrot's census of the Mobile District records the names of white settlers on the Eastern Shore — Hollinger, Walker, Mims, and others who will shape the county's future.
1799
Baldwin County EstablishedThe Alabama Territory creates Baldwin County, named for Abraham Baldwin, U.S. Senator from Georgia. Blakeley becomes the first county seat.
1813
Fort Mims MassacreCreek War forces attack Fort Mims, killing hundreds of settlers and triggering Andrew Jackson's campaign into Alabama. Baldwin County men serve in the war.
1820
County ReorganizedAn act extends Baldwin County boundaries and fixes Blakeley as the courthouse town, with Cyrus Sibley, Francis B. Stockton, and others named as commissioners.

Chapter Two

The Shape of the Land

Place-Names, Spanish Trails, and the Roads That Time Made

Baldwin County, Alabama

Hand-drawn map published in the Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly, Volume VII, No. 2 — January 1980

The land of Baldwin County — that long peninsula between Mobile Bay and the Perdido River, stretching south to the Gulf of Mexico — earned its character as much from its rivers and ridgelines as from any act of legislature. Before roads were surveyed on section lines, the Indians, the Spanish, and the first settlers all traveled the same route: the high ground above the headwaters of the creeks, where the mud stayed manageable and bridges were not required.

John Haupt of Elberta, a remarkably observant amateur historian, walked this land as a boy at the turn of the twentieth century and observed the last remnants of what was a Spanish Trail from Pensacola. Even in those years, he could see the trail as "a road strip where crops would not easily grow due to the many years of compaction by trail traffic." He traced its origins on foot and by map, following the high ground above the headwaters of the creeks and rivers.

"The present day roads that wind and bend — originally to keep the ox and horse-drawn wagons out of the mud — tend to be new roads, usually straightened to accommodate the modern motor car, on top of old roads." — Camp Powell and the Spanish Trail, Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly, Vol. VI

The Spanish Trail crossed Perdido Bay from Pensacola, starting on the beaches just below the present Lillian Bridge. It proceeded west along existing Highway 90, turning north approximately three miles east of Elberta — passing within a few hundred feet of Camp Powell, a mile north and east of Elberta on County Highway 87. Musket balls were found in the area in Haupt's childhood days, and the site was known as a Civil War-era Confederate encampment and recruitment ground.

The trail continued northwest, generally along Highway 59 through Robertsdale. A critical crossroad emerged at what became known as Greenwood — appearing on Civil War-era maps of 1865 and 1872, then disappearing, leaving only Greenwood Cemetery west of Loxley and Greenwood Branch northeast of that same town to mark where it once stood.

The Place-Names of Baldwin County

A 1974 initial survey of Baldwin County's special features catalogued the place-names that had accumulated over three centuries of settlement. Some came from the Spanish and French colonial periods. Others marked the farms, landings, and homesteads of the families who cleared the land. Still others — Tensaw, Perdido, Tombigbee — preserved the indigenous words that described what the land itself was.

Bay Minette, the county seat since the railroad era, anchors the north. Spanish Fort guards the entrance to Mobile Bay across the causeway. Daphne, Montrose, Fairhope, and Point Clear line the eastern shore, each with its own character. Foley and Elberta hold the central flatlands. Gulf Shores and Fort Morgan guard the barrier island at the county's southern tip.

The development pressure that came with the late twentieth century alarmed historians who had spent their lives walking this land. "The mauling of the hills around Spanish Fort and D'Olive Creek," one writer noted, "are the most obvious examples of this unplanned growth. Only drastic and rapid measures will blunt this trend." The warning was written in the 1970s. Some of those places are gone now.

Chapter Three

Communities

Sonora, Fairhope, Magnolia Springs, and the Towns That Grew

Before Sonora was named, there was not much of anything in that area but great forests. This began to change when Andrew Wynn, who lived east where Summerdale now is, went across Fish River to the Nimrod Underwoods and married their daughter, Ethel. Andrew and Ethel homesteaded 160 acres of timberland — land now partly owned by the Rhodes, Wynn, and Malec families — and it cost him six dollars.

The great pecan trees that once shaded their homestead still stood in the Rhodes field at the time this was written, in 1978, just east of the present Malec home. Those trees were the first witnesses of the community that would grow around them.

"We mention these names because they were the first white people in what was later to become Sonora." — Sonora Community History (1874–1978), Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly, Vol. VI, No. 4

The Making of Sonora

The story behind one homestead is worth telling. When Wynn's daughter Zariffa was to be married to Eldredge Gulledge of the Gateswood community, Eldredge had given her $400 as a dowry — a custom common at the time. One night before the wedding, a man came to the door and announced he was going to homestead the 160 acres where the old Gulledge house now stands. In the very early morning hours, Zariffa and her father rode to Daphne — where the Courthouse was located — and secured the homestead. As they went down the steps, the other man was just coming in. Too late.

By 1900, a Mr. Summers, a land agent for the Southern Plantation Development Company, had built a large two-story hotel and was attracting northern buyers to the area. Mr. Wilson opened a livery stable and traveled the trail road to Fairhope to meet incoming settlers — the only way to reach the area from Mobile being by bay boat. Some took one look and went back on the next boat. Others stayed.

The Post Office that gave the community its name was established by G. L. Sharretts in his home. Mrs. Sharretts was from Sonora, California, and that was the name she gave to the Post Office. In 1901, Sonora became a community. The mail came by boat up Fish River to Baldwin's Store and Landing, on the Boat Magnolia captained by Harmon Pierce, and was then carried by horseback to the Post Office.

The community was becoming what one writer called "a small United Nations" — Germans, Swedish, Norwegians, Polish, Lithuanians, and Czechoslovakians alongside the native-born. The Czechoslovakians came in 1921, brought by Joe Wesley, a land agent from Silverhill who had advertised in Czech newspapers about the opportunities in South Alabama.

In 1905, the L&N Railroad ran its line from Bay Minette down to Foley, and the town of Summerdale was born — named after Mr. Summers, who had brought most of the settlers to the area. The Railroad was called the Bay Minette to Fort Morgan City Line, but for reasons lost to history, it only reached Foley. It is said they ran out of money.

01

Fairhope

Founded in 1894 by a group of Single Tax colonists from Iowa led by E. B. Gaston, Fairhope embodied Henry George's radical land reform philosophy — all land held in common, taxes only on land value. The utopian colony attracted artists, intellectuals, and reformers from across the country to its bluffs overlooking Mobile Bay.

02

Magnolia Springs

A gracious resort community along the Magnolia River, known for its moss-draped live oaks and the artesian springs that gave it its name. Steamboats once called at its landing, and Northern visitors came to take the mineral waters. It remains today one of the last communities in the country to receive mail by boat.

03

Sonora

Named for Sonora, California, by the wife of its first postmaster, this community of Fish River homesteaders became a patchwork of immigrant families — Swedish, German, Czech, and Southern-born — who cleared the land, planted sweet potatoes and peanuts, and built a life among the longleaf pines.

04

Bon Secour

The name means "safe harbor" in French, and for generations of fishermen it was exactly that. At the mouth of the Bon Secour River on Mobile Bay's western shore, this community fished, oystered, and weathered the storms — including the catastrophic hurricane of 1906 that swept everything before it.

05

Blakeley

The original county seat, founded at the confluence of the Tensaw River and Mobile Bay, was briefly one of the most promising cities in the Alabama Territory. Yellow fever epidemics and the rise of Mobile ended its ambitions. The site is now preserved as Historic Blakeley State Park, where the last land battle of the Civil War was fought.

06

Malbis

Founded by Greek immigrants Jason Malbis and his associates in 1906, Malbis Plantation grew into a remarkable self-sufficient community with its own bakery, dairy, and the stunning Byzantine-style Malbis Memorial Church — a piece of the Aegean transplanted to the Alabama lowlands.

Clear Springs Church and Semirah Springs

Community life in rural Baldwin County revolved around the church, and the churches anchored communities long before the roads were paved. Clear Springs Church, a wooden structure built when the first families settled the high ground above Bon Secour Bay, was both sanctuary and civic center. On hot summer Sundays, the people would rise early, hitch up horses to wagons or buggies, pack a picnic lunch, and travel to Yupon where Sunday School was held under the giant oak trees. Following Sunday School, they would picnic and swim.

The Semirah Springs Freewill Baptist Church and Cemetery at Semirah Springs Community documented in the Historical Society's records preserves the names of the families who built this part of the county — names now found on the cemetery's stone markers, the last legible record of lives fully lived.

The school became the center of all community activities once it arrived. School only went through the seventh grade, and if a person attended that long, they became eligible to teach. There was no compulsory attendance, so many children attended only a few years. The first teachers were remarkable women — Miss Ethel Dodd, Miss Maynard, Miss Dora Owens — who shaped the minds of the children of the immigrant farmers and fishermen of the county.

Chapter Four

The Bay and the Gulf

Steamboats, Bay Boats, Hurricanes, and the Life of the Water

The eastern shore of Mobile Bay in Baldwin County had everything a settler could want — gentle climate, fertile soil, abundant seafood, and the most free and easy informal strip of land to be found anywhere. What it lacked was accessibility to Mobile. And so, since every venture has a reason, the bay boats were born.

Let us turn time back to the early 1800s, when early settlers and freight came by sailing vessels from Mobile to the Eastern Shore, or by ox teams and stage coaches from Pensacola. The earliest record of a steamboat on the Eastern Shore was the fifty-ton Emmeltinee, built at Alabama City — the site of today's Fairhope — that worked between 1827 and 1836 for service to large ocean-going ships barred from Mobile by a large sand bar extending from the mouth of Dog River.

An early unnamed steamboat is known to have made "over the bay" runs at a speed of four miles per hour between Mobile and Blakeley. She was so top-heavy that "to keep her from capsizing a huge cypress log was carried on one side as an outrigger." The Caloosa, one of the first steamboats built at Blakeley, had steam pressure so low that "a cannon had to be fired on deck to announce landings because blowing the whistle stopped the boat."

The Heroine — "slender as a toothpick and real speedy with a feathering bucket-type water wheel" — was built in Glasgow, Scotland, and used as a blockade runner during the Civil War, running from Bermuda to Wilmington to Havana and Fort Morgan from 1863 to 1865. Yankee blockade gunners complained: "Shoot at her bow and you miss her stern." — Steamboats to and From the Eastern Shore of Mobile Bay, submitted by Mrs. Kay Nuzum, 1979

Then came the Lucille, the Belen, the Crescent City, the General Lee, the Manatee, the Baldwin, and others. The first Fairhope was built by the Fairhope Industrial Association on the beach north of the old Casino at the foot of Fairhope Avenue. She was launched June 27, 1901, and went into regular service between Fairhope and Mobile on the first of September, making the 15-mile transit in an hour and fifteen minutes. Four years later she was destroyed by fire of unknown origin while moored at the Fairhope wharf.

The Eastern Shore, originally owned by the City of Boston, was considered by many the most beautiful of the bay boats with her yacht-like design, mahogany panelling, and brass railings. The Bay Queen, the largest and most elaborate of the fleet, were the last to ply between Mobile and Fairhope, especially on pleasure excursions.

The Landings of the Eastern Shore

At one time or another during steamboating days, a dozen or more landings lined the Eastern Shore of Baldwin County:

Starke
Dryer
Belrose
Daphne
Montrose
Sea Cliff
Volanta
Pier Street
Fairhope
Battles Wharf
Zundels
Point Clear (x2)

The Storm of 1906

In 1906, a devastating hurricane struck the entire Baldwin County coast. In the Sonora community, it demolished the Baptist Church on the Wynn property. A tide of nine-plus feet was recorded at the foot of the Mobile River. Cotton bales floated down the streets of Mobile. Between Choctaw Point and Three Mile Creek, fourteen vessels were sunk and thirty-four smashed ashore.

At Navy Cove — a small village four miles east of Fort Morgan — the story was catastrophic. Forty-seven people were stranded on a small knoll, the only dry land visible in any direction, without food or water, as the Gulf swept through. Houses disappeared. An entire family, clinging together as a wave hit, was thrown in every direction. One daughter survived by catching her hair in a bush as the waters swept her through the undergrowth. She held on through the night.

At Bon Secour, Clem Callaway's family escaped with only the clothes they wore. His wife Mary stayed up reading her Bible as the water rose. At 2:00 a.m. she heard a wave hit the house. The dog on the gallery was washed away and drowned. They left the house, the water above waist level as they went down the door steps, stumbling through fallen trees, before reaching shelter a quarter mile away. The next morning, the house was gone.

The great Gulf Beach that once had high sand dunes and large hammocks of trees was, after the storm, completely barren. Few trees have grown there since. The Gulf had washed all the sand dunes down. It came across the Lagoon and washed past where Fort Morgan Highway is today.

Chapter Five

The Civil War Years

Confederate Torpedoes, Oxford Rifles, and the Battle for the Bay

Confederate Naval Torpedoes

Among the more remarkable chapters of the Civil War as it played out in Baldwin County was the development and deployment of Confederate naval torpedoes — mines — in the waters of Mobile Bay. The defenses of Mobile Bay were elaborate, and they were tested when Admiral Farragut's fleet steamed in on August 5, 1864, with his famous order: "Damn the torpedoes. Full speed ahead."

The torpedoes were real. The monitor Tecumseh was sunk by one as it attempted to pass Fort Morgan. The Confederate defenders of Baldwin County's shore had turned the natural advantage of the bay — its shallow approaches, its treacherous sandbars, the powerful guns of Fort Morgan — into a formidable defensive system that held for most of the war.

The Oxford Rifles of the First Alabama Regiment mustered from the farms and communities of Baldwin County. The Spanish-American War of 1898 would later add another chapter, when Baldwin County men volunteered again — many of them sons and grandsons of the Confederate soldiers who had held the forts along the Gulf.

Life Through the War

For the families of Baldwin County, the Civil War meant privation, uncertainty, and the constant knowledge that Mobile Bay was a theater of active conflict. A Civil War letter preserved in the Historical Society's records — written by a Confederate soldier to family back home — conveys the texture of this time with startling intimacy.

The Confederate labor troops that built and maintained the fortifications drew on the enslaved population of the county. The accounting of that labor, in its bare administrative language, is among the most sobering documents in these archives.

Overton, described as "a Son of the South" in one account, represents the type of figure — the ordinary Baldwin County man caught between loyalty and survival — whose story the Historical Society worked to preserve before the last living memories of it passed away.

The Battle of Spanish Fort in April 1865 — sometimes called the Battle of the Village — was among the last significant engagements of the war in the Deep South. Union forces besieged and eventually took the Confederate fortifications at the strategic crossing point, effectively ending organized resistance in this part of Alabama.

"1810 and 1815 were stirring and transitional times in the history of Mobile and Baldwin. The Kemp Rebellion, the Creek War, the War of 1812–1815 with Great Britain must have made great demands upon the little settlements."
— From the Mervin Papers, as transcribed by Emma L. Roche, cited 1964

Andrew Lewis Holman — A Life in Full

Among the biographical portraits preserved in the Quarterly was that of Andrew Lewis Holman — a figure described as representing the full arc of life in this region across the transition from frontier to settled country. His account, drawn from family papers and the memories of those who knew him, captures the way ordinary people in Baldwin County lived through extraordinary times: clearing land, building churches, burying children, surviving storms, outlasting wars.

The Holman family, like so many in the county, arrived with very little and built everything from what the land provided — timber, turpentine, fish, the patient labor of families working together through the long years. Their story is repeated, with variations, in the genealogies of nearly every family in these pages.

Chapter Six

The Naval Stores

Turpentine, Timber, and the Longleaf Pine Economy

Baldwin County, Alabama, with its sub-tropical climate and abundance of longleaf (Pinus palustris) and slash (Pinus elliotii) pine trees — the chief source of American commercial gum turpentine — was most fortunate in using these renewable resources for the economy and well-being of its inhabitants. The industry that exploited this wealth was one of the oldest in the American South: the Naval Stores trade.

The first written history of the turpentine industry in Baldwin County was by William Bartram, in his wanderings in 1777. He described "three vast pots or kettles, each with a capacity of many hundred gallons — near the remains of an old fort or settlement, which he was informed were used for the purpose of boiling down the tar to pitch, there being vast forests of pine in the vicinity of this place."

The word "turpentine" (old terbentyne) derives from the Greek "terebenthina," originally meaning oleoresin. The crude oleoresin of pines — "Naval Stores," an old colonial name for the resinous products of pine used in the wooden ships of the British Royal Navy — was Baldwin County's economic backbone for generations.

Diagram of a Turpentine Still — illustrated for the Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly

Tools of the Turpentine Trade — dipper, collecting cup, hand tools, dip bucket, and a turpentine still

The Scale of the Industry

The first production of Naval Stores in Alabama was reported in the census of 1850 at a value of $17,800 to the state. By 1873, receipts in the Mobile market valued production at a total of $750,000. The entire state production grew to $1,109,760 in 1883. The ports of Mobile and Pensacola were major shipping points, and the world market was demanding — turpentine and rosin found use in paper manufacture, pharmaceuticals, paints and varnishes, soap, linoleum, adhesives, rubber, railroads, and shoe polish.

State-wide production began to decline in 1888, but the business remained a major source of income for Baldwin County for many years. By 1926, there were 134 turpentine operations in Alabama, and Baldwin County was the largest producing county, with forty operators. They ranged from the Bay Minette Turpentine Company to individual operators like W. H. Graham of Loxley, Everette Young of Magnolia Springs, and T. L. Taylor of Bromley.

The corporations formed around this industry in the early 1900s are preserved in the Baldwin County records: Bay Minette Naval Stores Co. (1904), Home Turpentine Company of Bon Secour (1912), Jennings Naval Stores, Owens Naval Stores of Gatewood, Dyas Naval Stores, Newport Turpentine and Resin Company, the O. K. Naval Stores Company of Seminole — and, in 1933, Malbis Plantation, a different kind of enterprise altogether.

"One corporation contract allowed the company to build, construct, maintain and operate a railroad for the transportation of logs, poles, lumber and freight of all kinds." — From the History of Naval Stores in Baldwin County, Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly, Vol. VIII

The Naval Stores Conservation Program

By the time the Naval Stores Conservation Program was established, the great forests of longleaf pine that had once covered Baldwin County were substantially depleted. The slash pine, faster-growing and more amenable to cultivation, was replacing the longleaf. The industry that had built so many fortunes and supported so many families was entering its twilight. The turpentine cups that had hung from countless pine trunks across the county's flatwoods were being cleared away, and what remained was mostly memory — preserved in the pages of this Quarterly.

Chapter Seven

Families & the Land They Left

Genealogies, Cemeteries, and the Names That Persist

The Baldwin County Historical Society's Quarterly was, at its heart, a genealogical project as much as a historical one. The family trees, genealogical extracts, cemetery records, and biographical sketches that fill its pages were the work of people who understood that the past was still living — in the names on gravestones, in the papers kept in family trunks, in the stories told by the oldest members of the oldest families.

The 1787 census of the Mobile District, conducted by Pedro de Favrot for the Spanish colonial government, is among the earliest records of the families who would become Baldwin County. The names already present — Hollinger, Walker, Mims, Jones, Nelson, Dyer, Johnston, Frazer — would echo through the next two centuries of the county's history. The Hollingers were already here. Adam Hollinger would later fight under Andrew Jackson in the Creek War.

Peter Randon stands as a figure bridging the Indian and settler worlds. Part Creek Indian by blood, he presented claims to the U.S. government for losses sustained when "hostile Creek Indians murdered his father and nearly every relative he had... in the massacre of Fort Mims." He was, in one letter's phrase, "the only man of that name I ever heard of anywhere" — a singular individual at the intersection of two colliding worlds.

The Cemeteries of Baldwin County

Documented in the Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly as sites of historical and genealogical record

Bear Point CemeteryOne of the earliest documented cemeteries in the county, preserving names from the first generation of settlers on the lower bay.
Witt Cemetery, Bon SecourA family cemetery at Bon Secour, among the oldest continuously maintained burial grounds on the western shore.
Episcopal Cemetery, Bon SecourAssociated with the early Episcopal congregation, this ground holds the remains of some of the county's earliest Protestant families.
East Foley CemeteryDocumented in Volume VI, representing the settler families of the central flatland communities.
Benton Cemetery, Bon SecourFamily cemetery preserving the Benton line and associated families from the lower Baldwin County area.
Lay Cemetery, MiflinA rural cemetery in the Miflin community, documented with painstaking record of each legible stone.
Swift Cemetery, MiflinAdjacent to Lay Cemetery, together these preserve the record of a close-knit rural community.
Shell Banks CemeteryLocated near the bay, this cemetery marks the site of an early settlement along the water.
St. Marks Lutheran CemeteryThe Lutheran congregation of the Eastern Shore, reflecting the German immigrant families who settled in the Daphne-Montrose area.
Vernant Park Baptist CemeteryAssociated with the Vernant Park community and its Baptist congregation, preserved in Volume VII records.
Pitman Cemetery AssociationAn active cemetery association as of the Quarterly's publication, preserving an early settler family's burial ground.
Semirah Springs CemeteryThe cemetery of the Semirah Springs Freewill Baptist community, documented with complete surviving records.
Timiney / So. River Park CemeteryAlso known as the South River Park Community Cemetery, this site preserves the names of the riverside settlement communities.
Hinote CemeteryA family cemetery preserving the Hinote line, documented in Volume IX with individual grave records.
Gulledge CemeteryAssociated with the Gulledge family of Sonora and surrounding communities, whose homesteading story is told in these pages.
Old Daphne Church CemeteryOne of the most historically significant cemeteries on the Eastern Shore, associated with the earliest Protestant congregation in the area.

Notable Figures Preserved in the Record

Among the biographical subjects given extended treatment in the Quarterly's volumes were Harry Toulmin, the judge and legal scholar who shaped the early law of the Alabama Territory; Elias Leonard Hammond, a figure connecting the early settlement period to the antebellum era; and Major Robert Farmer, the British officer who commanded Fort Conde in Mobile during the colonial period and whose actions helped define the boundaries of what would become Baldwin County.

The family of Sehoy — a Creek Indian woman who married a French officer named Marchand and whose descendants included some of the most prominent families of the Alabama and Mississippi Gulf Coast — is traced in a chart that demonstrates how deeply intertwined were the Indian and European bloodlines of this land. The Sehoy family tree connects Creek nobility, French colonials, British traders, and the founding families of Alabama in a single genealogical web.

Robert Jones, Charles Pitts — the names in these volumes are the names of the country itself: the roads bear them, the creeks carry them, the churches were built by their hands. The Baldwin County Historical Society's great project was to gather these names before the last people who remembered them were gone, to press them between the covers of a quarterly journal published from Fairhope, Alabama, and to trust that someone would read them someday.

You are reading them now.

BALDWIN COUNTY — A HISTORY IN STORIES

Drawn from the Baldwin County Historical Society Quarterly
Volumes VI through IX · 1973–1982
